question.text: Government Securities (G-Secs) are often called "gilt-edged securities" because they carry:

answer.text: The highest coupon rate among all debt instruments
Tax-free status on both interest and capital gains
Virtually no risk of default as they are backed by the government
Guaranteed returns that are always higher than inflation

explanation.text: **The Logic:** G-Secs are called "gilt-edged" because they carry sovereign guarantee, meaning there is virtually no default risk. The term originates from the gold-edged certificates historically issued by the British government. **The Trap:** "Highest coupon rate" is wrong — G-Sec coupon rates are generally lower than corporate bonds precisely because of their safety. "Tax-free" is wrong — only specific issues like tax-free bonds from PSUs have that feature. "Higher than inflation" is not guaranteed — real returns on G-Secs can be negative during high-inflation periods. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) tests understanding of G-Secs as part of the debt market. NISM Series V-A (Mutual Funds) also covers G-Sec funds. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When clients ask about "risk-free" investments, G-Secs are the closest benchmark — but remember they still carry interest rate risk and inflation risk, just not credit/default risk.
